1: Comfort, comfort my people, says your God. The doubled comfort that opens Isaiah's Book of Consolation (Isaiah 40-55) announces the divine urgency behind the gospel. Luke 2:25 records that Simeon was looking for the consolation of Israel — and found it when he held the infant Jesus. 2 Corinthians 1:3-5: God is the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction. The doubled word is the emphatic form in Hebrew — YHWH does not merely suggest comfort but commands it to be proclaimed. The command becomes gospel: the one who brings the consolation has arrived.
2: Speak tenderly to Jerusalem, and cry to her that her warfare is ended, that her iniquity is pardoned, that she has received from the LORD's hand double for all her sins. The pardon of iniquity and the end of servitude — the core gospel announcement. Colossians 2:13-14: God forgave all our trespasses, canceling the record of debt that stood against us with its legal demands, setting it aside, nailing it to the cross. Romans 5:20: where sin increased, grace abounded all the more. The double-for-all-sins formula anticipates the NT pattern: the judgment that fell on Christ was the receiving of humanity's debt, so that the double-forgiveness could be proclaimed.
3: A voice cries: In the wilderness prepare the way of the LORD; make straight in the desert a highway for our God. All four Gospels cite this verse of John the Baptist (Matthew 3:3; Mark 1:3; Luke 3:4; John 1:23) — making Isaiah 40:3 the most explicitly Messianic verse in the Baptist's commission. The way of the LORD that the voice prepares is Christ himself: John 14:6 — I am the way, the truth, and the life. The highway in the desert is both the return of the exiles and the coming of the Messiah; John the Baptist is the hinge where both meanings meet.
4: Every valley shall be lifted up, and every mountain and hill be made low; the uneven ground shall become level, and the rough places a plain. The eschatological leveling — exaltation of the lowly, humbling of the proud — is the social signature of the kingdom. Luke 1:51-52 (Mary's Magnificat): he has scattered the proud in the imagination of their hearts; he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate. The Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12) enact this literally: the poor in spirit, the meek, the mourning are the valleys lifted up. Christ's entry into Jerusalem on a donkey (not a war horse) is the leveling of imperial pomp.
5: And the glory of the LORD shall be revealed, and all flesh shall see it together, for the mouth of the LORD has spoken. The universal revelation of YHWH's glory to all flesh. John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father. Luke 3:6 quotes verse 5 explicitly in the context of John the Baptist's ministry: all flesh shall see the salvation of God. 2 Corinthians 4:6: God has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ. The incarnation is the revelation of YHWH's glory to all flesh.
6: A voice says, Cry! And I said, What shall I cry? All flesh is grass, and all its beauty is like the flower of the field. 1 Peter 1:24-25 quotes verses 6-8 directly in the context of the gospel: All flesh is like grass and all its glory like the flower of grass. The grass withers, and the flower falls, but the word of the Lord remains forever. And this word is the good news that was preached to you. The contrast between perishing flesh and the enduring word is the contrast between every human institution and the gospel of Christ. The announcement of human transience is the necessary preface to the announcement of the eternal Word.
7: The grass withers, the flower fades when the breath of the LORD blows on it; surely the people are grass. The Spirit-breath of YHWH that withers flesh-grass is the same Spirit who gives life (John 6:63: it is the Spirit who gives life; the flesh profits nothing). The Spirit that withers the proud flesh is the Spirit that raised Jesus from the dead (Romans 8:11: if the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you...). The same divine breath that judges the transience of all flesh is the breath breathed on the disciples: receive the Holy Spirit (John 20:22).
8: The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God will stand forever. The eternal word standing against the transience of all flesh — Peter's citation (1 Peter 1:25) identifies this word as the good news that was preached to you. Hebrews 1:10-12: the heavens and earth will perish and be changed, but the Son remains the same and his years have no end. The word that stands forever is the person of Christ (John 1:1-2: the Word who was in the beginning with God), and his resurrection is the proof that this word cannot be silenced by death.
9: Go on up to a high mountain, O Zion, herald of good news; lift up your voice with strength, O Jerusalem, herald of good news; lift it up, fear not; say to the cities of Judah, Behold your God! This is the root of the NT word gospel (euangelion, the good news announced from a high place). The command to say Behold your God! is the fundamental gospel proclamation — John 1:29: Behold, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world! The announcement is not a doctrine but a person: look, God himself has come. The herald on the high mountain who lifts her voice without fear is the model for every apostolic proclamation.
10: Behold, the Lord GOD comes with might, and his arm rules for him; behold, his reward is with him, and his recompense before him. YHWH coming with his arm — Isaiah 53:1 asks to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? and answers: in the Servant's suffering. Revelation 22:12: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me, to repay each one for what he has done. The Lord GOD who comes with might and reward is identified in Revelation as the returning Christ: the arm of YHWH revealed in the cross is the same arm that executes final justice.
11: He will tend his flock like a shepherd; he will gather the lambs in his arms; he will carry them in his bosom, and gently lead those that are with young. Jesus appropriates this image explicitly: John 10:11 — I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep. John 10:16: I have other sheep that are not of this fold; I must bring them also... there will be one flock, one shepherd. Matthew 18:12-14: the shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the one. The gathering of lambs in arms is the incarnation itself — God taking fragile human beings into his embrace. The gently-leading of nursing mothers is the pastoral character of the Messiah who does not break a bruised reed (Isaiah 42:3).
12: Who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand and marked off the heavens with a span, enclosed the dust of the earth in a measure and weighed the mountains in scales and the hills in a balance? The incomparable power of the creator — measuring oceans in a palm, weighing mountains on scales. Colossians 1:16-17: all things were created through him and for him... and in him all things hold together. John 1:3: all things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made. The creator who weighs the mountains is the one who became the carpenter from Nazareth — the one through whom the mountains were made holding a hammer.
13: Who has measured the Spirit of the LORD, or what man shows him his counsel? Whom did he consult, and who made him understand? Romans 11:33-35 quotes this verse: For who has known the mind of the Lord, or who has been his counselor? Or who has given a gift to him that he might be repaid? 1 Corinthians 2:16 resolves the question: who has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him? But we have the mind of Christ. The incomprehensible divine wisdom that no human advises becomes knowable in Christ: in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3).
14: Whom did he consult, and who made him understand? Who taught him the path of justice, and taught him knowledge, and showed him the way of understanding? The self-sufficiency of divine wisdom — YHWH consulted no one, was taught by no one. Jesus embodies this in John 7:15-16: the crowd marvels that he knows letters without being taught — and Jesus says my teaching is not mine, but his who sent me. The unschooled wisdom of YHWH becomes the unschooled wisdom of the rabbi from Galilee who confounds the teachers. Yet he is the source of all wisdom, not its recipient.
15: Behold, the nations are like a drop from a bucket, and are accounted as the dust on the scales; behold, he takes up the coastlands like fine dust. The nations as a drop from a bucket before YHWH. Revelation 19:15: Christ strikes the nations with the sharp sword that comes from his mouth and rules them with a rod of iron. The powers that seem overwhelming are less than a water-drop before the Lord of all. This reframes every apparent political or military crisis facing the covenant community: the empire that threatens is dust on the scale before the one who gathers the lambs in his arms.
16: Lebanon would not suffice for fuel, nor are its animals enough for a burnt offering. The created order's entire resource cannot constitute adequate worship of YHWH. This sets up the NT's theology of Christ's sacrifice as the only sufficient offering. Hebrews 10:4-10: it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins... Jesus Christ offered his body once for all. Lebanon's forests — the greatest ancient symbol of natural abundance — are not enough for YHWH's altar. Only the self-offering of the Son constitutes worship that YHWH accepts and that makes atonement for sin.
17: All the nations are as nothing before him; they are accounted by him as less than nothing and emptiness. The nullification of national power before YHWH's sovereignty. 1 Corinthians 1:28: God chose things that are not, to bring to nothing things that are — so that no human being might boast in the presence of God. The cross enacts this perfectly: the crucified Messiah — accounted as nothing by Rome and the religious authorities — is the one who brings the powers to nothing. The nations that are less than nothing before YHWH face the Lamb at the final judgment (Revelation 20:11-15).
18: To whom then will you liken God, or what likeness compare with him? The incomparability question — which is also a question about the incarnation. John 1:18: no one has ever seen God; the only God, who is at the Father's side, he has made him known. The answer to what likeness will you compare to God? is the face of Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6). God's likeness is not gold or stone (Acts 17:29) but the face of the Son — the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15). The incomparability of YHWH is not a barrier to knowing him; it is the reason the incarnation is the only adequate revelation.
19: An idol! A craftsman casts it, and a goldsmith overlays it with gold and casts for it silver chains. The idol that must be fastened so it does not fall — manufactured, dependent, immovable without human hands. Acts 17:29: we ought not to think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and imagination of man. Hebrews 12:27-28: everything that can be shaken will be removed; what remains is the unshakable kingdom. The idol chained to its pedestal is the anti-image of the risen Christ who moves where he will (John 20:19: Jesus comes through locked doors) and who holds all things in place by the word of his power (Hebrews 1:3).
20: He who is too impoverished for an offering chooses wood that will not rot; he seeks out a skilled craftsman to set up an idol that will not move. The poverty-grade idol — the poor man's substitute for living God. Even the best the idol-maker can produce is an immovable piece of incorruptible wood. The contrast with the incorruptible Christ: Acts 2:27 quotes Psalms 16:10 — you will not let your Holy One see corruption. Christ is the only truly incorruptible one — not manufactured from wood that will not rot, but the one whose body by divine power did not decay in the tomb.
21: Do you not know? Do you not hear? Has it not been told you from the beginning? Have you not understood from the foundations of the earth? The rhetorical appeal to creation-witness. Romans 1:20: his invisible attributes, namely his eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made, so they are without excuse. The witness of creation that leaves humanity without excuse is the same witness that prepares them for the fuller revelation in Christ. The creation-knowledge is never sufficient on its own, but it testifies to the one who made all things and who became flesh.
22: It is he who sits above the circle of the earth, and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers; who stretches out the heavens like a curtain, and spreads them like a tent to dwell in. YHWH enthroned above the earth's circle — the creator's perspective from above, where inhabitants are grasshoppers. Colossians 1:16-17: all things were created through Christ and for Christ, and in him all things hold together. The one who sits above the circle of the earth is the one through whom the heavens were stretched (John 1:3) — and he chose to enter the grasshopper-sized perspective of human existence (Philippians 2:7: he emptied himself, taking the form of a servant).
23: Who brings princes to nothing, and makes the rulers of the earth as emptiness. The nullification of human rulers before YHWH's sovereignty. Luke 1:52: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate. 1 Corinthians 2:6: Paul speaks wisdom not a wisdom of this age or of the rulers of this age, who are doomed to pass away. The rulers who are brought to nothing are the powers and principalities that Christ disarmed at the cross (Colossians 2:15). The political theology of Isaiah 40:23 is the lens through which the NT reads every human empire.
24: Scarcely are they planted, scarcely sown, scarcely has their stem taken root in the earth, when he blows on them, and they wither, and the tempest carries them off like stubble. The swift withering of the planted-and-blighted rulers. James 4:14: what is your life? For you are a mist that appears for a little time and then vanishes. The rulers who appear to take root are themselves grass (vv6-7) — and the same breath that withers the grass withers the throne. Matthew 24:35: heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away. The withered rulers are contrasted with the enduring word of Christ.
25: To whom then will you compare me, that I should be like him? says the Holy One. The Holy One's own declaration of incomparability — and the Holy One is identified in the NT as Jesus (Mark 1:24: I know who you are — the Holy One of God; John 6:69: Peter's confession). The incomparability of YHWH takes surprising form in the incarnation: God is not like an idol precisely because he became what no idol can become — a human being who suffers, dies, and rises. The Holy One who says to whom will you compare me? is the one who compared himself to a grain of wheat that dies to bear much fruit (John 12:24).
26: Lift up your eyes on high and see: who created these? He who brings out their host by number, calling them all by name; by the greatness of his might and because he is strong in power, not one is missing. The creator who names every star and loses none. John 10:3: the sheep hear his voice, and he calls his own sheep by name. John 17:12: I kept them in your name... and not one of them has been lost. Hebrews 1:3: Christ upholds the universe by the word of his power. The creator who calls each star by name and keeps none missing is the shepherd who calls each sheep by name and ensures none is lost — the same sovereign care governs both the cosmos and the covenant community.
27: Why do you say, O Jacob, and speak, O Israel, My way is hidden from the LORD, and my right is disregarded by my God? The complaint that YHWH has forgotten his people. Psalms 22:1: my God, my God, why have you forsaken me? — the lament that Jesus prays on the cross (Matthew 27:46). The theology of divine hiddenness that this verse addresses is the experience of the cross: from the outside, the cross looks like YHWH disregarding the right of his Son. Both Psalm 22 and Isaiah 40:27 set up the divine answer: YHWH has not forgotten, has not disregarded — and the resurrection is the proof.
28: Have you not known? Have you not heard? The LORD is the everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth. He does not faint or grow weary; his understanding is unsearchable. The everlasting, unwearied creator. Hebrews 13:8: Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever. Revelation 1:8: I am the Alpha and the Omega, says the Lord God, who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty. John 4:6 records that Jesus was weary from his journey — the incarnation is the mystery of the unwearied God taking on human weariness, so that those who grow weary might receive the strength that comes only from the one who never does.
29: He gives power to the faint, and to him who has no might he increases strength. The divine power given specifically to the weak and failing. 2 Corinthians 12:9-10: my grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness... when I am weak, then I am strong. Philippians 4:13: I can do all things through him who strengthens me. The strength given to the faint is the Spirit of the risen Christ poured out on the church — the same Spirit who raised Jesus from the dead dwelling in mortal bodies (Romans 8:11) gives resurrection-life to those who have no might.
30: Even youths shall faint and be weary, and young men shall fall exhausted. The universal limitation of human strength — even youth and vigor cannot sustain themselves indefinitely. Romans 5:6: while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly. The incarnation is timed to the moment of maximum human exhaustion: when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son (Galatians 4:4). Christ comes not when humanity is strong but when every youth has fainted — when the world has demonstrated that no human strength can solve the human problem.
31: But they who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles; they shall run and not be weary; they shall walk and not faint. The promise of eagle-renewed strength for those who wait on YHWH. Acts 1:4-8: Jesus commands the disciples to wait in Jerusalem for the Holy Spirit — and the Spirit poured out at Pentecost is the eagle-renewal of Isaiah 40:31. Romans 8:26: the Spirit helps us in our weakness. Galatians 5:25: if we live by the Spirit, let us also keep in step with the Spirit. The waiting posture that receives renewed strength is the posture of faith in Christ — and the wing-like renewal is the first fruits of resurrection life available now through the Spirit.
